summary: Gruber defends Apple's decision to disable Internet-wide music sharing in iTunes 4.0.1, arguing it was a rational response to third-party tools like iLeech and iSlurp turning iTunes into Napster-style piracy software. He acknowledges the feature wasn't accidental but characterizes it as a social experiment that failed when abuse put it in direct conflict with the iTunes Music Store. He methodically rebuts critics like Cory Doctorow and Steve Mallett, pointing out factual errors in their arguments while maintaining that Apple's move only prevented iTunes itself from being a piracy tool, not P2P sharing in general. Gruber frames the choice as pragmatic: Apple could either disable the feature or wage a costly, foolish legal battle with the RIAA.
key_points: iTunes 4.0's Internet sharing was intentional, not accidental — Apple's own help documentation described how to connect to specific IP addresses outside local subnets
Third-party apps like iLeech and iSlurp turned iTunes into copying software, putting Internet sharing in direct conflict with the iTunes Music Store
Apple's decision prevented iTunes from being used as a piracy tool, but did not prevent users from sharing music via other software
Cory Doctorow mischaracterized the iTunes 4.0.1 release notes as calling the removal an 'enhancement' when Apple clearly listed it as a separate clause
The iTunes Music Store was not yet a 'major revenue stream' — at a million songs per week it represented roughly $15 million quarterly against $1.475 billion in total revenue
money_quotes: I'm baffled — not by Apple's decision, but by much of the reaction to it.
Any industry that expresses outright hostility toward its most enthusiastic customers is in trouble.
Apple is not the RIAA, nor is Apple in any position whatsoever to dictate to the RIAA.
Choose your battles wisely, the saying goes, and regardless of righteousness, a P2P battle against the RIAA would be just about the most foolish thing Apple could do.
There is no logical way you can claim that iTunes could act, simultaneously, as an RIAA-sanctioned front end to the ITMS, and as bootlegging software the RIAA deems illegal.
Apple's decision was not about preventing bootlegging; it was about preventing the use of iTunes as a bootlegging tool. There is a huge difference.
It's a world I don't want to live in. — Terrific. Might I suggest starting here?
key_insight: Apple's removal of Internet sharing from iTunes was not capitulation to the RIAA but a rational act of self-preservation — you cannot simultaneously sell music and distribute it for free through the same application.
